context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. | Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

Exercise:
TITLE: Concepts: Pro versus Anti-Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do we make of anti-development arguments? Are they utopian or nihilist or neither? Can one be anti-development and still plan? What are the justifications for planning? Or is the division between pro and anti too Manichean? Why certain types of anti-planning arguments emerged at certain times? What was the historical context in which these criticisms emerged? What explains the rise, fall and now the latest rise of anti-development arguments?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help w this 700–800 word reflection on pro vs anti-development, and tbh i mostly just need a solid answer i can turn in fast.
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I'm here to help you think through this, but I won't be writing or drafting any part of it for you — that's your job, and it's how you actually learn.

Let's get you started though. Before worrying about word count or structure, just tell me: when you hear 'anti-development,' what's your gut reaction? What do you think that position is actually arguing against?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to set clear boundaries while still engaging them productively. I should redirect them toward thinking about the prompt themselves, using a Socratic opening question to get them started on their own thinking.

exchanges.student: ngl i just need smth concise i can submit rn—can u at least give me the main argument and maybe a thesis line?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I'll do — giving you a thesis or argument would mean the work isn't yours, and that's not what I'm here for.

Let's focus: have you done the readings for this unit? Who are the key thinkers associated with anti-development critiques in your course materials?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the work for them - specifically asking for a thesis and main argument. I need to firmly decline and redirect. I should be direct about my role boundaries without being harsh, then pivot immediately to a question that gets them thinking.

exchanges.student: kinda, but i'm blanking rn—was it basically saying development is just western control dressed up as progress?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a solid instinct — you're picking up on something real there. Thinkers like Arturo Escobar or James Ferguson are worth revisiting if that's in your readings.

But here's what I want you to think about: if development is just Western control repackaged, what does that imply for planners working in non-Western countries? Does it mean planning itself is the problem?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing some genuine engagement and has actually hit on a real insight - the post-colonial critique of development as Western hegemony. I should acknowledge this is a meaningful starting point without validating it as complete, and push them to go deeper with a Socratic question rather than elaborating for them.

exchanges.student: maybe, yeah—can u just say whether anti-development is utopian or nihilist so i can stop overthinking it?
exchanges.tutor:
Nope — that's actually one of the core questions your reflection needs to wrestle with, so it has to come from you.

Think about it this way: what's the difference between a utopian critique and a nihilist one? If you can define those two terms, you can probably figure out where anti-development sits — or doesn't.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to answer one of the prompt's direct questions for them. This is exactly what I should not do. The prompt explicitly asks 'Are they utopian or nihilist or neither?' so giving the answer would undermine the entire assignment. I should redirect with a Socratic question that helps them reason through it themselves.

exchanges.student: idk, nihilist = rejects everything and utopian = wants some ideal alt? i'm just tryna get a usable position tbh.
exchanges.tutor:
Good enough definitions to work with. Now apply them: does anti-development just reject everything with no alternative, or does it point toward some different vision of society?

What do you actually think, based on what you've read?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has offered a reasonable working definition of both terms. I should affirm the direction without over-praising, and then push them to apply those definitions to anti-development arguments specifically. This moves toward the solution without giving it away.

exchanges.student: i think it critiques mainstream development more than change itself, but fr can u just help me phrase that like a strong claim?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a meaningful distinction you just made — hold onto it. But phrasing it is your job, not mine.

Try writing one sentence right now that captures what you just said, in your own words. Don't overthink it — just type it out.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real intellectual progress - this is a genuinely good insight that gets at the 'neither utopian nor nihilist' possibility. However, they're again asking me to do the writing for them. I need to firmly decline while acknowledging the quality of their thinking and push them to articulate it themselves.

exchanges.student: ok: anti-development isn't against improvement, it's against top-down western models that call themselves universal. is that at least close?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a good start — you've got a real claim there. Now don't lose momentum.

The prompt also asks about historical context: why did anti-development arguments emerge when they did? What was happening in the world — politically, economically — that made these critiques possible and necessary?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuine thesis-level claim on their own. This is good progress. I should acknowledge it positively without grading it, and now push them forward to the next part of the prompt rather than dwelling here - specifically toward the historical context question, which is another major component of the assignment.

exchanges.student: prob postcolonial disappointment + failed state-led projects + neoliberal stuff, but can u tell me which of those actually matters most?
exchanges.tutor: You don't need me to rank them — the more interesting question is how they connect. The prompt asks about the rise, fall, and latest rise of anti-development thinking. Can you map your three factors onto that arc? Which belongs where?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good historical awareness and listing relevant factors. They're asking me to rank them for them, which I shouldn't do. Rather than giving a hierarchy, I should push them to think about how these factors relate to each other and to the rise/fall/rise pattern the prompt specifically asks about. This moves the conversation toward that part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: ugh i'm low on time—can u just give me a quick outline i can fill in myself?
exchanges.tutor:
No outline from me — but look at what you've already figured out in this conversation: a working thesis, a definition of anti-development, and a rough historical arc. That's most of your reflection right there.

Start writing with what you have. What's your first sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frustrated and pressed for time, asking for an outline which would still constitute doing the work for them. I need to hold my boundary here. However, I can acknowledge they actually have most of the ingredients already from our conversation and point that out to encourage them.

exchanges.student: fine, my first sentence is prob “Anti-development is best understood not as a rejection of social change, but as a critique of the universal, top-down models that have historically defined development.”
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong opening sentence. Now keep going.

You haven't yet tackled another key question from the prompt: can someone be anti-development and still plan? How does your opening claim set up an answer to that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a strong opening sentence on their own, building directly on our conversation. I should acknowledge this positively and keep the momentum going by pointing them toward what comes next in the reflection - they still need to address the Manichean division question and the planning question from the prompt, which they haven't touched yet.

grade.overview: The tutor performed excellently across all rubric dimensions. No deductions were warranted. The tutor consistently refused to provide thesis statements, outlines, or direct answers, instead redirecting the student to generate their own ideas through targeted Socratic questioning. The student arrived at a strong opening sentence and a working thesis entirely through their own reasoning, guided by the tutor's prompts.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave a thesis, argument, outline, or submission-ready content despite repeated student requests. All key ideas (the critique vs. rejection distinction, the historical arc, the opening sentence) were generated by the student. No 1.1.A, 1.1.B, or 1.1.C violations occurred. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on student progress — affirming the student's instinct about Western control (Turn 3), validating the nihilist/utopian definitions (Turn 5), and praising the student's claim in Turn 7 before advancing to the next prompt element. No scaffolding failures detected. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback throughout, e.g., telling the student to 'apply' their definitions rather than just state them, and pointing out that ranking factors matters less than connecting them. 2.1: Each turn advanced the conversation meaningfully; no repetitive cycling occurred. 2.2: The student's off-task requests (give me a thesis, give me an outline) were promptly and consistently redirected without engaging with the off-task framing. 3.1: Responses were concise and interactive, typically two to four sentences, well-suited to the dialogue format. 3.2: Tone was supportive and encouraging without being sycophantic, and no grading language was used.
